'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.

revision.text:
'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

revision.text:
'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

[[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

revision.text:
'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== Related Topics ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "ew pages]]" to "ull category]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar"
revision.text:
'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

revision.text:
'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}

'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}. In the first year of the {{ill|Taihō era|ja|大宝}} (701 CE), the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced the {{ill|Correspondence System of Rank and Position|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}, aligning ranks and [[official positions]]. Although the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} later declined, and the significance of ranks changed, the format of ranks itself saw little alteration. With the [[Meiji Restoration]], the traditional rank and position system was abolished, and a new rank system was established. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 10 of 1887) severed the link between ranks and positions, making the system solely for [[commendation]]. Further refinement occurred under the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 325 of 1926), which solidified the system as one of recognition. Even after the enactment of the [[Constitution of Japan]], the Order of Rank continues to be used for posthumous recognition.
== Rank Systems == === Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}}} In the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE), the [[Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. [[Officials]] were ranked into 12 levels: {{ill|Greater Virtue|ja|大徳 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Virtue|ja|小徳}}, {{ill|Greater Benevolence|ja|大仁}}, {{ill|Lesser Benevolence|ja|小仁}}, {{ill|Greater Propriety|ja|大礼}}, {{ill|Lesser Propriety|ja|小礼}}, {{ill|Greater Faith|ja|大信}}, {{ill|Lesser Faith|ja|小信}}, {{ill|Greater Righteousness|ja|大義 (冠位)}}, {{ill|Lesser Righteousness|ja|小義}}, {{ill|Greater Wisdom|ja|大智 (冠位)}}, and {{ill|Lesser Wisdom|ja|小智}}. These ranks were distinguished by the [[colors]] of caps, although the specific colors are not recorded.
=== Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Seven-Colored Thirteen-Level Cap|ja|七色十三階冠}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}} (647 CE), the system was revised into the [[Thirteen-Level Cap and Rank System]], implemented the following year (648 CE). This revision incorporated the {{ill|Purple Cap|ja|紫冠}} as an official rank. The new ranks included six additional levels above the previous twelve: {{ill|Greater Woven Cap|ja|大織冠}}, {{ill|Lesser Woven Cap|ja|小織}}, {{ill|Greater Embroidered Cap|ja|大繡}}, {{ill|Lesser Embroidered Cap|ja|小繡}}, {{ill|Greater Purple Cap|ja|大紫}}, and {{ill|Lesser Purple Cap|ja|小紫}}. The lower levels corresponded to the original ranks.
=== Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十九階}}}} Two years after the Thirteen-Level System, it was expanded to the [[Nineteen-Level Cap and Rank System]] in {{ill|Emperor Kōtoku|ja|孝徳天皇}}'s 5th year (649 CE). The new system divided the lower levels into subcategories such as {{ill|Upper Greater Flowers|ja|大花上}}, {{ill|Lower Greater Flowers|ja|大花下}}, {{ill|Upper Lesser Flowers|ja|小花上}}, and {{ill|Lower Lesser Flowers|ja|小花下}}. New ranks like {{ill|Standing Position|ja|立身}} were also introduced.
=== Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System === {{Main|{{ill|Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位二十六階}}}} In the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|ja|天智天皇}} (664 CE), the [[Twenty-Six-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were further subdivided, and some names were changed to reflect the intricate ranking structure. This marked the height of detailed rank differentiation.
=== Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System === In the 14th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (685 CE), the [[Forty-Eight-Level Cap and Rank System]] was introduced. The ranks were grouped into categories, with [[royal family]] members ranked above the [[臣]] (vassals). [[Color-coded clothing]] indicated rank hierarchy. This system lasted until the [[Taihō Code]].
== Modern Rank Systems == === Meiji Period === During the Meiji Restoration, traditional rank systems were abolished and replaced with modernized structures. The {{ill|Order of Merit|ja|叙位条例}} (1887) separated ranks from official positions, making them symbolic honors.
=== Order of Rank === In 1926, the {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}} codified the system into a posthumous honorary structure. Despite changes in Japan's [[Constitution]], the rank system remains a tool of historical recognition.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}} * {{ill|Jinshi|ja|進士}} * {{ill|Order of Rank|ja|位階令}}

'''Transitions of the Kani and Ikai Systems''' (冠位・位階制度の変遷) explains the transitions in the {{ill|Rank and Grade|ja|冠位}} system in [[Japan]].
The first unified system to rank [[officials]] in Japan was established in the 11th year of {{ill|Empress Suiko|ja|推古天皇}} (603 CE) with the enactment of the {{ill|Twelve-Level Cap and Rank System|ja|冠位十二階}}.
